author: @Leo99929
text: Why don't you just measure the RPM with a tachometer, if you're only interested in voltage (effectively RPM)?

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text: I agree with this comment. It would also be more accurate, as the voltage the way  Bob measures it depends on how close he holds the coil to the magnets.

replies.author: @Leo99929
replies.text:
@leostarling5746 Not great scientific method being demonstrated. He doesn't have to go to the Nth degree, but plugging in an ammeter and using a set coil location would go a long way. 

I'd wager it's because his magnetically levitated bearing introduces some oscillation based on the reaction, so since the volt meter is "infinite impedance" it won't load up the rotor, and he can blame variation on his hand movement. 

It wouldn't be that hard to rig up an Arduino with a volt meter, amp meter, and use a hall effect or IR sensor to get RPM. Then he could just plop different heads on to the base and get instant comparable logged results. 

Then he could just copy and paste them into an excel sheet and overlay performance graphs. 

Something useful to work on whilst his 3D printers work.

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text: @Leo99929 the real problem is that better measurements will show that leaf rotor under Darwin to be rather poor.

author: @monadyne
text:
I don't understand this at all. The amazing virtue of the feathered disk is that when wind blows against it horizontally, it raises the feathers. This provides a large area of wind resistance, allowing for capture of a significant amount of the wind's thrust. As the disk spins, after having captured its share of the wind's force, the feather flattens down for the return, thus drastically reducing its resistance to the wind. That was the incredible breakthrough of this design. Why abandon that for capturing downward force?

Has it been demonstrated that the same volume of air blowing downward produces more voltage that when it moves across the wind? Is this because the only volume of wind being captured by the disk in horizontal position is equal to the LWH area of the feather, as compared to the volume of wind hitting the entire area of the disk?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: yeah just more area

author: @RobertA-hq3vz
text: I don't think you can test these things with just open circuit voltage. As soon as you put a load on the generator the generator starts applying a retarding torque to the fan blade. Which causes it to lose velocity fast, which causes the open circuit voltage to collapse.  You need to test these things with a small resistive load. The test of any wind plant is not how much voltage you get out of it but how much power you can extract from the wind. Edit>   unless you output the voltage to a super capacitor which dumps power to a load when the voltage reaches a predetermined level.

author: @senorjp21
text: Open circuit voltage isn't a meaningful measurement of power. Measuring voltage drop across a resistor will let you calculate power using the formula V^2 / R (voltage in volts squared divided by resistance in ohms = power in watts)

author: @kevinbuiied
text:
I do enjoy the fact that you go ahead and "do" the proof of concepts. It would be a step better in my opinion if you utilized a set of standardized tests to gauge actual improvement. An example would be a standardized serpentine coil winding around a specific array of magnets that you keep consistent across various motor/turbine designs. Or having a target RPM for a given design. Measure the generated voltage and current to get a power reading over time. The various videos I've watched have leant towards showing the quality/ideas of energy production and not so much the quantities involved in the energy production. Exhibiting a voltage difference doesn't necessary equate to high energy production.

Sure, one could say that I go ahead, make and measure these systems myself. I definitely could, but it doesn't seem worth it to me to buy all the materials necessary to make any of these things, especially when there isn't substantial evidence of it being effective. And like... you've already got your prototype made.

With a couple of video sections focused on testing, I think you could really amp up the discussion in these comments.

Thank you for exploring these energy topics.

replies.text: I agree - but I do that - I just don't view everything - you would be amazed how dull that is lol

author: @HollisInman
text: My friend... I discovered your channel while conducting my own micro wind turbine research... So I have a high level of respect for you as you experimentally validated a lot of what I was researching and confirmed some of my own experimental results. That being said, I stopped watching this video when you said the words, "diverting wind". Unless that happens over an airfoil in a very narrow way, that means losses. Go back to the Ugrinsky and start playing with "baffles" at low aspect ratios. I think you'll be pleased.

text: Not entirely convinced by VentumDynamics, their performance data is virtually non-existent... 🤔 The design looks cool certainly, and does capture the imagination, but in general I've yet to be convinced by the whole omni-directional advantages of a VAWT when you can simply point a HAWT towards, and optimise for, what will always largely be a prevailing wind.... 😋

replies.author: @nb6175
replies.text: I agree. The company seems a bit weird. Also worth noting VD had this design long before this channel started talking about Darwin's design. In spirit, it's really about saving space and having the moving expensive parts being small. The most efficient solution will always be a gigantic turbine facing into the wind etc. This solution is about increasing the surface area of wind you're catching without having to change the size and direction of the actual expensive/sensitive turbine. Whether it does any better than other designs is the question, and it's not one VD have really provided any concrete figures on. Frustrating this channel is fixated on vague measurements and relative improvements rather than measuring over all power output that would take away all doubt that comes from constantly rebuilding both the turbine and the generator specs.

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: well - clearly they don't feel the need to convince you and clearly they have convinced those who matter lol

replies.author: @saiello2061
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I've seen this kind of thing many times before, performance data obfuscation by marketing spin that's too "on the nose". It's almost formulaic. I found some numbers, not on their front page, but right at the bottom of a downloadable PDF that suspiciously corresponds closely to Betz law calculations. They even state that these numbers are largely theoretical. Why? Does that not raise a red flag for you...? The only people they need to convince are those non-engineers, those that have money to spend for the green initiative. These days, that's like taking candy from a baby... 😁

author: @timucinbahsi445
text: no no no. go back. the feather thing was better :) seriously though, the main point of darwin design is collecting more air but vawts are already good at that. with the feather configuration eliminating resistance on the opposite side, it's the best of both worlds

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: that depends on what you mean by better - in what terms?

replies.author: @timucinbahsi445
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering i'm mostly thinking using the space efficiently. especially the floor area the generator occupies. achieving that without a rotating top heavy structure is the main advantage of the darwin turbine. that's why we love it.

however, the feather configuration, if added an upside down turbine to catch the wind on the other side of the crossection, could even be better at exactly the same aspects. i'm also assuming the counterweight on fins idea is adopted for them to require minimum force opening up.

it uses the entire footprint, it can still have both generators at the bottom, can be stacked up to utilize more of the vertical space and for the efficiency, the way i'm eyeballing it is that i imagine how much slower the air is when leaving the turbine and how much of it do we slow down.

also, because the wind is pushing the fins in the same direction it blows, it can generate a much larger torque. if you throw a CVT between the turbine and the magnets, you can have the turbine stiffness respond to the wind speed.

most importantly, i'm so glad to find your channel. seeing a nice person having created an environment to have fun exploring engineering questions is always a happy portion of the day. cheers

author: @alanwakefield2453
text:
Hi Rob, not really relevant to this video, but when I was a lad back in the early sixties my first introduction to motors was  the Ladybird Book of Magnets Bulbs and Batteries. This was my older brothers book and I read it from cover to cover avidly. 
I proposed that with a horseshoe coil at the top of my bike forks and magnets around the rim of the wheel I could produce more power than a standard bike dynamo. This was poo pooed by brother and physics teacher as impractical because the magnets would be too heavy to be practical. So I wonder, if when you are looking for a side line project while waiting for something to print you could perhaps, with modern magnets test out my hypothesis?

replies.author: @whatelseison8970
replies.text:
I hope you don't mind my chiming in, but I'd have to say the answer is that it was absolutely impractical with ferrite magnets and an incandescent lamp, but with modern magnets and an LED light you could most likely at least get _some_ light out of such a setup.  I think rather than using it for a headlight though it might be cool to lay a serpentine coil along the rim with stationary magnets on the forks, then connect that to strings of LEDs inside the wheels which would flash as you ride. 
Depending on how many wiggles your coil has and how many strings you use you could have patterns that rotate backwards as you ride forwards, for example.

Honestly though, if you want a worthwhile headlight you're far better off simply using a battery.  A few watts may not seem like much, but it really robs your ability to coast or maintain speed.  I used to use a pair of old 18650's and a 12V LED bulb meant to replace the halogen bulbs in desk lamps.  Even in the cold it'd last me several rides.  The bulbs are quite cheap and they have an integrated regulator that can take in 7-12V, AC or DC so they're always lit at full brightness.  When the battery drops below 7V it cuts out completely so you won't damage your cells by running them flat if you forget to shut it off.  Mine were 6W and I think and they cost 4 CAD.  The style is MR16 IIRC.

Just food for thought you can make hydrogen from methane and there's more methane on earth than gas an oil combined. 
Formula H2 (hydrogen) + CO2 = CH4 (methane)  , so when hydrogen leaks to atmosphere it'll combine with 2 CO molecules which makes CH4..
To separate ch4 methane use a CO2 filter there your left with H2 , CO2 filters are lithium filters used Apollo moon mission and in operating theatre for patients breathing recirculation,  not sure how long co2 filter will last , BUT maybe Rob could revisit with options of co2 /carbon felt I haven't dived any deeper.
Not sure how methane over copper an UV light would go splitting it to hydrogen. 
All food for thought cheers.

replies.author: @Merkasaur
replies.text: @paddy2661 hi Patrick. I appreciate the effort of your response, but it seems to have no relevance to the topic of my comment. TnT did a series of videos on urea electrolysis. A process that theoretically produces more energy than it consumes, and produces Hydrogen and Nitrogen gas. This could be done with human urine or industrially produced urea. It seems to be an efficient process but a required reagent is Potassium Hydroxide (KOH). This becomes carbonated to produce K2CO3 and you have to put more KOH in to continue the electrolysis. Without recycling this reagent, the process becomes incredibly expensive for the energy density of the hydrogen

author: @richardglickman7203
text:
1) In this test the airflow was through (vertical to) the turbine. The feather turbine is designed for the airflow across (horizontal to) the turbine. How does it work with both vertical and horizontal airflow simultaneously? Which is what you wold get when combining the Darwin and feather systems.
2) Up to now, your tests involved small blades which appeared to result in greater area for airflow and a smaller area for capturing the airflow. This was the first test reversing this ratio by using large blades and reducing the space between the blades. Would adding vertical fins to the outer edges of the blades improve performance by capturing air bleeding off the outer edges?

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: calling me sad is pretty arrogant mate and not a little insulating - ah well - and you have really missed the important things here - that is the Darwin system, generation at the Rim and maglev - to be honest the rotor design is pretty much secondary

replies.author: @martinsmallridge4025
replies.text:
@ThinkingandTinkering Thanks for the reply Robert... The bit I'm unsure about here is that it seems inefficient in two ways. 

1. Normally a wing blade would mean air hits the blades from the leading edge whereas the Darwin design is effectively pushing air perpendicular to the leading edge so to my untrained eye that seems like a lot of waste energy.

2. The blades being hinged seems somewhat redundant, again because the air is coming at the blades from above, thus forcing the blades to stay down rather than rotating upwards.

That said, I'm a great believer in the mantra of "If it's stupid and it works it ain't stupid" but I am trying to consolidate my experience and limited knowledge to understand things properly..

replies.author: @leostarling5746
replies.text: @ThinkingandTinkering I called that particular rotor design sad, not you. I have no quarrel with the generation at the rim and maglev. I have not missed them, been following your videos for quite some time and am on your side. I mentioned the Water's turbine because imho it is far superior to these leaves in many ways. After all, it was spinning pretty well when you held it to the wind. I will not go into more details of why it is better, as I have no wish to upset you any more.

author: @marcfruchtman9473
text:
Hello Robert, I agree that you need to reconsider the aerodynamics of the blade. The current method is definitely sub-optimal with probably a huge proportion of the wind energy wasted. There is no need for a hinged blade in that configuration because all of the wind in a Darwin system will be perpendicular to the disc, where as without the Darwin, the disc spins perpendicular to the wind, and so it needs to have the blades flap closed when the blade comes into the wind. That won't be needed in the Darwin system, so the fan blades should always be oriented to catch the wind. 
I really like the idea of the Darwin system especially with the downward flow, because it make repair of the fan and motor very easy! Thanks for your hard work!

replies.text: when air going thru feathers, it tries to lift them to make a bigger hole to pass .. besides shape, weight of feathers is pushing air down trying to make that hole smaller .. with barely no friction on its axis the air pushing feather up and feather weight pushing down causes movement

replies.author: @ravenmad9225

author: @uscitizen5656
text: A little less than 5 MPH and you were getting 16V. That is a definite game changer in wind turbine technology. I'm going to have to make 1 of these to augment, my solar panels to charge my hydroponic batteries for the water pumps

replies.author: @ThinkingandTinkering
replies.text: go for it mate

author: @brianreynolds4627
text: WOW!…Generating over 12 volts that’s brilliant!…can’t wait for the final version that you’ll be happiest with…it would be great at that point if you were able to make them as kits to purchase from you…as many folk I’m sure, like myself, won’t have 3D printers…just a thought…I’d certainly be interested in purchasing one from your good selves…love the Vids and your presentations…always a joy to see what’s next…thank you for taking the time and effort to make and post them…Brian.

replies.text: Not exactly free because of the materials needed to create the generator, but Robert and Luke are definitely developing better solutions than we've seen in the past with smarter and safer designs.

replies.text: High rise buildings kill magnitudes more birds than wind turbines do.

replies.author: @justtinkering6713
replies.text: They say if they paint one wind turbine blade black, the bird kill goes way down.

replies.author: @RandomAxeOfKindness
replies.text: Coal smog particulates kill orders of magnitudes more birds per watt generated, but bird strikes are one of many arguments against gigantic wind turbines and in favor of smaller, more distributed generation.
